$schema_notes: Internal reference file for the Rise XLIFF Translation Tool showcase + Gumroad sales page. Read this file at the start of any session where you're editing the showcase, drafting Gumroad copy, or revising the tool's marketing. It is the single source of truth for tool framing. Update it whenever copy decisions change so future sessions don't drift.

do_not_reopen: MediaFire as host (Drew set this between sessions, not negotiable)
Public repo / public showcase page (private Pages requires Enterprise)

thesis.headline: Translation is for humans, not machines.
thesis.expanded: Machines should do what they do well — parse XML, reformat structure, validate output. Humans should do what they have done since the Rosetta Stone — read meaning, write meaning, in the language they speak. The tool inverts the typical CAT-tool workflow that forces humans to wrestle with tags: it handles the structure so the translator never sees XML.
thesis.consequence_statement: The pool of possible translators expands from 'can work with XLIFF' to 'can work with a spreadsheet.' That is the entire value proposition in one line.

tool.what_it_is_one_line: A browser-based tool that lets machines parse Articulate Rise 360's XLIFF so humans can translate in Excel, CSV, or any CAT tool — then rebuilds the translated XLIFF for re-import.
tool.what_it_is_paragraph: Articulate Rise 360 lets you export a course for translation as an XLIFF (.xlf) file. The export is technically standard XLIFF but structurally awkward: Rise wraps paragraph and inline content in <g> tags that CAT tools can't visually hide, leaving translators with walls of tagged text. This tool sits between Rise and the translator: it parses the XLIFF, extracts just the text into a clean Excel spreadsheet with three plain-language markers (** [BR] [P]) and built-in instructions, then takes the completed work and rebuilds a valid XLIFF for re-import to Rise. Works with Excel directly, with CSV, or as a clean input for any CAT tool the translator prefers.

rise_xliff_specific_problems.summary: Rise's XLIFF export is technically standard but structurally hostile to translation workflows that don't use specialized XLIFF tooling. The tool exists because the gap between Rise's free XLIFF export and Articulate's paid translation service is wider than it should be.
structural_issues: Rise wraps multi-paragraph and inline content within each segment in <g> tags. CAT tools treat all <g> tags as inline formatting that must be preserved verbatim, but Rise uses them for both paragraph structure and inline emphasis. The translator can't easily tell which tags are safe to ignore.
Manual translation of the raw XLIFF is the most common cause of failed re-imports according to Articulate's own community support staff.

Common downstream symptoms reported in the Articulate community: silent import failures where the course re-imports without error but stays in the original language, dropped paragraph structure, and formatting drift.
Translators without dedicated XLIFF tooling — including in-house bilingual staff — frequently cannot process the file at all and have to outsource.
rise_xliff_specific_problems.business_context: Articulate offers a paid translation service. XLIFF export is the alternative for those who don't pay for the service. The XLIFF's awkward structure functionally pushes small teams toward paying.
this_tool_solves_by: Parsing the <g>-tag structure programmatically so the translator never sees it.
Reducing all relevant formatting to three plain-text markers: ** for bold, [BR] for line break, [P] for paragraph break.
Pulling each translatable segment onto its own clearly-typed spreadsheet row.
Validating on reassembly that markers and rows came back intact before the developer re-imports to Rise.
sources_consulted_during_writing: https://www.worldaccent.com/blog/2021/02/translating-rise-360.html — translation agency's structural critique of Rise XLIFF including the <g>-tag issue

https://community.articulate.com/discussions/rise-360/urgent-problem-of-translation-in-rise — Articulate community thread; staff comment confirms manual XLIFF translation is the most common failure mode
https://community.articulate.com/discussions/rise-360/new-in-rise-export-for-translation — Articulate's own launch announcement, source of the 'it's that easy!' phrase echoed in the page copy

primary_value_props: Translators stay in the tool they know — Excel, CSV, or any CAT tool — and never see XLIFF or XML.
All formatting reduces to three plain-text markers (** [BR] [P]) that any translator can preserve without training.
Built-in marker validation and missing-row detection catch translator errors before the file goes back to Rise.
The pool of possible translators expands from 'can work with XLIFF' to 'can work with a spreadsheet.'
Zero install. Runs entirely in the browser. Course content never leaves the developer's machine.
secondary_value_props: Works as a CAT-tool bridge: translators with Trados, memoQ, Phrase, MateCat keep their TM and glossary, but get cleaner inputs than Rise's raw XLIFF.
Spreadsheet has a separate Instructions tab so translators don't need a separate brief.
Segment type labels (title / heading / body / button / hint / card front / card back / prompt) give translators context for each row.
Column D in the spreadsheet adds per-row warnings (⚠️ KEEP ** for bold, ⚠️ KEEP [P]) on rows that need them.
Reassembly validates the final XML before download — broken XLIFF is caught before it reaches Rise.
Works on any OS with a modern browser. No subscription, no per-seat license.

audience.framing_paragraph: Built for the case where the right translator is the one who knows the domain, not the one with the CAT tool. NGOs and non-profits with bilingual subject-matter experts on staff. Health programs whose community workers speak both languages and know the material. Faith-based, legal aid, and advocacy programs translating training for the communities they serve. The tool lets a Rise course developer choose a translator by domain expertise instead of by software stack.
primary_segments: Articulate Rise 360 developers translating content where accuracy of meaning matters more than throughput speed
Small L&D teams without a dedicated localization workflow, with content worth translating well
NGOs and non-profits with bilingual subject-matter experts already on staff
Public health, legal aid, faith-based, and advocacy programs translating training for the communities they serve
Independent instructional designers choosing translators by domain fit
Translators who prefer Excel — or who use a CAT tool but want cleaner inputs than Rise's raw XLIFF
pain_signals: Asking translators to edit XLIFF directly and watching them refuse or struggle
Paying Articulate's translation service for a small course because the DIY path is too painful
Manually copy-pasting text out of Rise into Word for translation
Losing bold, italic, or paragraph formatting after a translation round-trip
Paying for a CAT tool licence solely to translate a single course
Having a domain expert (community health worker, social worker, attorney) on staff who is bilingual but cannot use translation software

steps.title: In Rise
steps.description: Click Settings (upper right) on the Rise course. Open the Translations tab. Make sure 'Keep HTML formatting' is checked. Export the course as XLIFF. You get a .xlf file.

steps.title: In the tool
steps.description: Open the tool in any browser. Click the file area to add your .xlf. The tool parses it, shows every translatable segment with its content type, counts the formatting that needs preserving, and offers a download of the translator-ready spreadsheet.

steps.title: In Excel, CSV, or a CAT tool
steps.description: Open the spreadsheet. Instructions tab first, Translation tab second. Translate in Column C. Don't worry about XLIFF, don't worry about tags. Keep the ** / [BR] / [P] markers around the equivalent translated words. That's the whole job. The same spreadsheet works as CSV input for any CAT tool that reads CSV.

steps.title: Back in the tool
steps.description: Click to add the original .xlf and the completed spreadsheet (XLSX or CSV). The tool rebuilds the XLIFF with translated targets, validates the result, and offers a translated .xlf you re-import to Rise.

tabs.name: Instructions
tabs.purpose: Plain-language rules for the translator. How to fill in, what the markers mean, what not to change.

columns.contents: Source text with ** around bold and [BR] / [P] for breaks
Content type label (title, body, heading, button, hint, card front, card back, prompt, description) plus per-row warnings (⚠️ KEEP ** for bold, ⚠️ KEEP [BR], ⚠️ KEEP [P]) on rows that need them

features_detailed.name: Segment type labels
features_detailed.description: Every row in the spreadsheet is tagged with its content type — title, heading, body, button, hint, card front, card back, prompt — so the translator understands context.

features_detailed.name: Formatting markers
features_detailed.description: Bold becomes **bold text**. Line breaks become [BR]. Paragraph breaks become [P]. Translators just keep the markers around the equivalent translated words.

features_detailed.name: Built-in instructions tab
features_detailed.description: The downloaded spreadsheet has an Instructions tab with the rules in plain language. The translator doesn't need a separate brief.

features_detailed.name: Per-row marker warnings
features_detailed.description: Column D notes the rows that contain bold or paragraph breaks, with a ⚠️ KEEP reminder where it matters.

features_detailed.name: Marker validation
features_detailed.description: If the translator dropped a [P], [BR], or ** marker, the validation panel calls out the exact row before reassembly downloads.

features_detailed.name: Missing-row detection
features_detailed.description: If the row count in the spreadsheet doesn't match the segment count in the XLIFF (translator skipped rows, added rows, deleted rows), the tool reports the mismatch with specifics.

features_detailed.name: XML integrity check
features_detailed.description: Final XLIFF is parsed to confirm it's well-formed XML before download. If something would break Rise's import, you find out here.

features_detailed.name: Extract stats
features_detailed.description: Total segments, segments with bold, with line breaks, with multi-paragraph content. Helps scope and quote a translation job accurately.

features_detailed.name: CAT-tool compatible
features_detailed.description: Spreadsheet output works with Trados, memoQ, Phrase, MateCat — anything that reads CSV or XLSX. The translator keeps their TM and glossary; the tool just gives them cleaner inputs than Rise's raw XLIFF.

features_detailed.name: Runs in the browser
features_detailed.description: No server, no upload, no account, no install. Course content stays on the developer's machine.

removed_features_do_not_revert.name: Two-tab workflow
removed_features_do_not_revert.reason_removed: Drew clarified: the tool's two tabs (Extract / Reassemble) are navigation, not a marketing point. The two tabs worth highlighting are inside the OUTPUT spreadsheet (Instructions + Translation). That's now covered separately under 'Built-in instructions tab' and 'Spreadsheet structure'.

removed_features_do_not_revert.name: Drag and drop
removed_features_do_not_revert.reason_removed: Drew reports drag-and-drop is not working in practice on his end. The marketing copy now uses 'click to add files' or 'click the file area' instead. Tool code is NOT being changed.

technical_specs.runtime: Modern desktop browser (Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Safari)
technical_specs.install: None. Single HTML file, open and use.
technical_specs.data_handling: 100% client-side. Files are read in the browser via the File API. Nothing is uploaded to any server.
technical_specs.dependencies: SheetJS (XLSX library) loaded from cdnjs CDN for reading and writing .xlsx files and .csv files
input_formats: XLIFF 1.2 as exported by Articulate Rise 360 (.xlf extension)
output_formats: .xlsx (Excel spreadsheet for the translator, with Instructions and Translation tabs)
technical_specs.tested_in: Rise 360 XLIFF exports observed during development
known_limits: Designed specifically for Rise 360 XLIFF structure. Other tools' XLIFF exports may not parse identically.
Translator must keep the formatting markers (**, [BR], [P]) in place. The tool reports markers that were dropped but cannot reconstruct intent.
Drag-and-drop file input may not work in all browser/OS combinations — use the click-to-browse path for reliability.

alternate_distribution_via_gumroad.notes: The same description, screenshots, and feature copy in this JSON should drive the Gumroad product page when Drew is ready.

headline_drafts: Translation is for humans, not machines.
The Rise 360 translation tool built for the translator, not the CAT tool.
Your Rise course, translated by anyone who can use a spreadsheet.
From 'can work with XLIFF' to 'can work with a spreadsheet.'
subhead_drafts: A browser-based bridge between Articulate Rise 360 and any translator. Excel, CSV, or any CAT tool. No XLIFF, no XML, no install.
Built so course developers can choose their translator by domain expertise instead of by software stack.
The translator never sees XML. The course developer never re-imports a broken XLIFF.
bullet_points_for_sales_page: Works with any Articulate Rise 360 course exported as XLIFF
Translator works in Excel, CSV, or any CAT tool — no XLIFF training needed
All formatting reduces to three plain-text markers: ** for bold, [BR] for line breaks, [P] for paragraph breaks
Built-in spreadsheet Instructions tab so translators don't need a separate brief
Per-row warnings flag rows with bold or paragraph breaks (⚠️ KEEP **)
Marker validation catches dropped formatting before re-import to Rise
Missing-row detection catches translator skips before they reach the course
XML integrity check confirms the rebuilt XLIFF is well-formed
Bridges to Trados, memoQ, Phrase, MateCat — anything that reads CSV or XLSX
Runs 100% in the browser — course content never leaves your machine
No install, no account, no subscription

objection_handling.why_not_a_real_cat_tool: If you localize courses every week, a CAT tool is worth the setup. If you do one course a quarter and your translator is happiest in Excel — or your translator is a bilingual domain expert on staff who doesn't own Trados — this is the tool.
objection_handling.what_if_my_translator_uses_a_cat_tool: The spreadsheet imports cleanly to any CAT tool that reads CSV. Their translation memory, glossary, and QA still apply. They just get cleaner inputs than Rise's raw XLIFF — three plain-text markers (** [BR] [P]) instead of <g>-tag soup.
objection_handling.is_it_safe: It runs entirely in your browser. No server, no upload, no telemetry. Open the HTML in any modern browser and the tool is just JavaScript reading files on your machine.
objection_handling.what_if_the_translator_messes_up_the_mar: The Reassemble step tells you exactly which rows are missing **, [BR], or [P] markers, AND tells you if rows were skipped entirely, before you re-import to Rise. You fix the spreadsheet, not a broken course.
objection_handling.why_should_i_pay_for_this_instead_of_usi: Articulate's translation service is a managed offering — you hand them the course and pay for completion. This tool is the alternative when you want to choose your translator (domain expert, in-house bilingual staff, trusted freelancer) but don't want to subject them to raw XLIFF.
